ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried fish food product class; no specific fried fish head entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried fish generically. No specific fried fish head code exists; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically for fried fish head exists in the clinical terminology. Not applicable.

main_ingredients: fish head (typically snapper, grouper, or carp)
allergens: gluten (if wheat flour used in coating)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of fried fish head in Singapore is approximately 250 g, representing half or a whole smaller fish head, served as a shared dish or individual main course.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, snapper, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (USDA SR Legacy) adapted for deep-fried fish head with coating and oil absorption
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for fried fish head exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked snapper (dry heat, NDB 15230) with upward adjustment for deep-frying oil absorption (~8–10% fat increase per standard frying model) and coating contribution. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is the closest defensible approximation available from an approved source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried fish head. GI estimated at 38 based on analogous battered/fried fish entries (GI ~38, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The low GI reflects the dominant protein and fat content with minimal carbohydrate from the thin coating. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 12.0) / 100 = 4.6; however, using 250 g serving with 4.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g gives 12.0 g carbohydrate per serving, GL = 38 × 12.0 / 100 = 4.6. Rounded to 2.3 per half-serving equivalent for a 125 g portion context. GL per 250 g serving = 4.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce marinade and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving of fried fish head contributes approximately 800 mg sodium, representing about 35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced soy sauce and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 38 and only 4.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, fried fish head has a low glycemic impact and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments and in moderate portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat; fish head provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the fish head itself provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, deep-frying adds significant total fat (approximately 11 g per 100 g) and saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and prefer steamed or poached fish head preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried fish head (炸鱼头, Zha Yu Tou) is a popular Chinese and Singaporean dish in which a whole or halved fish head — most commonly from snapper (red or pink), grouper, or carp — is marinated in soy sauce, salt, white pepper, and sometimes ginger or garlic, lightly coated in cornstarch or rice flour, and deep-fried in hot oil until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior flesh remains moist and tender. The dish is a staple in Chinese seafood restaurants, zi char (煮炒) stalls, and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia. The fish head is prized for its gelatinous cheek meat, collagen-rich skin, and the fatty, flavourful flesh around the jaw and collar, which many diners consider the most delicious part of the fish. Nutritionally, fried fish head is a high-protein, moderate-fat food providing approximately 18.5 g protein and 11.2 g fat per 100 g, with a caloric density of around 195 kcal per 100 g. It is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and omega-3 fatty acids, though deep-frying increases the saturated and total fat content compared to steamed preparations. The sodium content is moderate to high due to soy sauce seasoning, making portion control advisable for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is low (approximately 38), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation. The dish is typically served as a shared main course alongside steamed rice and vegetables in a family-style meal setting.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for fried fish head was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked snapper with a standard deep-frying oil absorption model. GI is estimated from analogous battered fried fish entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is well-supported and the food composition is straightforward.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked snapper (NDB 15230) with deep-frying oil absorption adjustment (~8–10% fat increase). Carbohydrate contribution from thin cornstarch/rice flour coating estimated at approximately 4–5 g per 100 g of finished product. Sodium estimated based on typical soy sauce marinade usage in Chinese zi char cooking. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy snapper data adjusted for frying; these are not directly measured values for this specific dish. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on battered fried fish entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. GL per 250 g serving = 4.6. The is_halal flag is set to true as fish is generally halal; however, the use of soy sauce should be verified for halal certification in specific restaurant contexts. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient fields are populated with estimated values from the adapted USDA source.
